---
title: "The deviance bias of congenial multiple imputation, with applications to likelihood-ratio comparison and model selection"
subtitle: "JAIGP submission — working draft (§1–§6 in draft prose as of 2026-06-04; references auto-generated from the bibliography)"
author:
  - name: "Marcus Waldman"
    note: "ORCID-verified human prompter of an AI-assisted derivation; provenance per JAIGP norms (see the statement at the end)."
date: last-modified
bibliography: ../literature/mi-spectral-better-bibTeX.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Draft status.** This is the submission document in progress. The canonical *sourced
derivation* — every external claim pinned to a source passage, every number pinned to a
committed artifact, executable verification inline — is the companion document
`manuscript/derivation.qmd`; this paper states results and points there for the full
provenance apparatus. All sections are now in draft prose; the references list is generated
automatically from the bibliography.
:::

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Multiple imputation answers a practical question with an elegant division of labor: an
imputer fills in the missing values several times from a model for the complete data, an
analyst fits whatever model they care about to each completed data set, and simple rules
combine the results [@rubinMultipleImputationNonresponse1987]. For point estimation and
variance the combining rules are settled. For *likelihood-based* quantities they are not.
The averaged log-likelihood across imputations — the Q-function of the EM algorithm in its
infinite-imputation limit, and the computational backbone of the modern MI
likelihood-ratio tests of @mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b and
@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022 — is *not* the observed-data log-likelihood, and it
is not an unbiased stand-in for the complete-data log-likelihood either. Deviances built
from it are systematically too favorable.

This paper derives exactly how favorable. For a regular likelihood model fit on
congenially imputed data, with an estimated scale or covariance, the averaged
log-likelihood at the observed-data maximum likelihood estimate overstates its
complete-data target by
$$ \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) \;+\; (A){+}(C), $$
where $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ is the trace of the relative-increase-in-variance
matrix — the total odds of missing information about the model's parameters — and
$(A)+(C)$ is an $O(1)$ design-imbalance term that vanishes under MCAR and is nonzero under
MAR exactly when the missing and observed units differ on the conditioning variables. The
half-trace itself decomposes into two parts with opposite signs and distinct mechanisms:
an *imputation-bias* term $+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (imputed data flatter the
model that imputed them) and an *estimation-mismatch* term
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ (the MI fit targets the complete-data
maximizer, which is not the observed-data one). The decomposition, the MAR term, and the
scope fence — a known-scale, location-only fit has no such bias — are stated as the main
theorem in @sec-theorem and derived in full in the companion document.

Two applications give the bias teeth.

**Likelihood-ratio comparison.** When two nested models are fit to the *same* imputations,
almost everything cancels — but not everything. At the null the deviance numerator carries
a differential bias equal to $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp)$, the missing
information of the tested directions projected in the *complete-data* metric — provably
*not* the difference of the two models' own trace corrections, which always overstates it.
The same sharing of imputations collapses the per-data-set noise of the comparison from
$O(\sqrt n)$ to $O(1)$, and cancels the design-imbalance term exactly at the null. At the
null, this differential bias is precisely the mean that a correctly calibrated reference
distribution already absorbs [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] — so tests built on such
references are safe; the bias bites where no reference stands between the statistic and
its consumer.

**Model selection.** Information criteria are exactly such a consumer: MI-AIC compares
penalized averaged deviances as numbers. The bias is model-specific — each candidate's
criterion is too small by its own $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ — so uncorrected
MI-AIC systematically favors the candidates with the *most* missing information about
their own parameters. In our pre-registered study, every single misclassification of the
uncorrected criterion lands on the largest-RIV candidate. Adding
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k)$ per candidate repairs the mean; as a penalty, the
repaired criterion reproduces the missing-data information criterion
$\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ of @shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017, whose surcharge is half
that of the earlier complete-data-discrepancy criteria
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a]. But the mean is not the whole story: we
derive, pre-register, and then measure a three-step contrast — matching the mean, the
first two moments, or the entire per-model null distribution of the criterion — and show
that the achievable target splits cleanly along the null/noncentral axis: the null side
calibrates *completely* (in the pre-registered design the distribution-matched criterion
selects indistinguishably from complete-data AIC), while no observable transform can
restore the evidence that missing data destroyed, and the two stronger corrections
actively shrink it.

**What is new, and what is not.** The trace *penalty* for model selection is not new — it
is Shimodaira's $\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$, and the doubled version goes back to
@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a and Shimodaira's own PDIO. The calibration of
MI *test* references is not new either — that is the Meng–Rubin line
[@mengPerformingLikelihoodRatio1992b] culminating in @chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022
and @chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022, which this work sits upstream of rather than competes
with: calibration makes the reference right, the present correction makes the statistic
referred to it centered. What is new here: (i) the *decomposition* of the deviance bias
into imputation bias and estimation mismatch, with the estimated-scale scope fence;
(ii) the MAR design-imbalance term $(A)+(C)$, beyond the fixed-pattern settings of the
information-criterion literature; (iii) the extension from the deterministic EM pivot to
proper multiple imputation; (iv) the exact differential bias of the MI likelihood-ratio
numerator — the complete-data-metric projection, the proof that the naive difference of
trace corrections always overstates it, the $O(1)$ paired-noise collapse, and the exact
null cancellation of the design-imbalance differential; and (v) the pre-registered
distributional-matching contrast for model selection, with its honest null/noncentral
split. Uncongenial settings [@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a] are out of
scope throughout: congeniality is assumed, and the bias derived here is what remains
*after* the imputer and analyst agree.

@sec-theorem states the setup and the theorem. @sec-lrt-app and @sec-ic-app develop the
two applications. @sec-numerics reports the pre-registered numerical studies. @sec-disc
discusses scope, engine-dependence, and open directions. Throughout, the companion sourced
derivation (`manuscript/derivation.qmd`, rendered alongside this paper) carries the full
proofs, the source-passage audit trail, and the executable symbolic verification; this
paper is self-contained at the level of statements and evidence.

# Setup and main theorem {#sec-theorem}

## Objects {#sec-objects}

Let $Y$ collect $N$ independent draws from a regular parametric model with parameter
$\theta \in \mathbb{R}^k$; we instantiate throughout with the $p$-variate normal,
$\theta = (\mu, \operatorname{vech}\Sigma)$. An ignorable MAR mechanism hides some
coordinates of some units, leaving $Y_{\text{obs}}$. Three log-likelihoods organize
everything: the complete-data $\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta)$ (unavailable), the observed-data
$\ell_{\text{obs}}(\theta)$ with maximizer $\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$, and the averaged
log-likelihood over imputations drawn from the congenial model at
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ — in the infinite-imputation limit, the EM Q-function
$$ \bar Q_\infty(\theta) =
\mathbb{E}\big[\ell_{\text{com}}(\theta) \,\big|\, Y_{\text{obs}},\, \hat\theta_{\text{obs}}\big], $$
the deterministic-FIML form whose finite-$M$, proper-MI version replaces the point
$\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}$ by posterior draws. The three Fisher informations satisfy
$I_{\text{com}} = I_{\text{obs}} + I_{\text{mis}}$ [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022], and
the relative increase in variance is $\mathrm{RIV} = I_{\text{obs}}^{-1} I_{\text{mis}}$,
whose trace is the total odds of missing information.

The object of study is the *deviance bias*
$$ T \;=\; \bar Q_\infty(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}) \;-\;
\ell_{\text{com}}(\hat\theta_{\text{obs}}), $$
the amount by which the averaged log-likelihood flatters the fit relative to what the
complete data would have shown at the same parameter value.

## The theorem {#sec-thm-statement}

::: {#thm-bias}
**(Deviance bias under congenial MI.)** Under the regularity, congeniality, and properness
conditions of the companion derivation, with an estimated scale or covariance,
$$ \mathbb{E}[T] \;=\; \underbrace{+\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{imputation bias}}
\;\underbrace{-\;\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})}_{\text{estimation mismatch}}
\;+\; (A){+}(C) \;+\; o(1)
\;=\; \tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) + (A){+}(C) + o(1), $$
where $(A)+(C)$ is an $O(1)$ design-imbalance term that is zero under MCAR and, under MAR,
nonzero exactly when the conditioning-variable distributions of missing and observed units
differ. With a *known* scale (location-only fit) the conditional missing-data entropy is
parameter-free and the bias collapses: $\mathbb{E}[T] = 0$ under deterministic FIML and
$-\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV})$ under proper MI.
:::

The proof — Term B (estimation mismatch) by an elementary Taylor argument, Term A
(imputation bias) by a second-order expansion turning on an entropy-Hessian cancellation —
is given in the companion derivation, together with the symbolic verification of every
trace identity and the Monte-Carlo confirmation of the total (the pooled pre-registered
estimate $\hat T = 2.43 \pm 0.26$ against the $(A)+(C)$-augmented prediction $2.42$–$2.55$;
only totals and paired contrasts are Monte-Carlo-tight at finite $n$, a candor note the
companion makes precise). The conditions are the standard ones for a regular likelihood — a
correctly specified, identifiable model with nonsingular information and an interior maximizer,
together with MAR-ignorable missingness and proper, congenial imputation — plus the
estimated-scale requirement above; the companion derivation states the full list and the role
each assumption plays.

# Application I: the likelihood-ratio differential {#sec-lrt-app}

When two models are compared by a deviance built from the *same* imputations, much of the
bias of @thm-bias cancels — but not all of it, and the part that survives is exactly the part
a likelihood-ratio comparison cares about. Write
$\hat d_L = 2[\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^*) - \bar Q_\infty(g(\hat\gamma^*))]$ for the MI deviance
between an unconstrained and a constrained maximizer of the shared $\bar Q_\infty$ — the
infinite-imputation form of the stacked-data likelihood-ratio statistic of
@chanMultipleImprovementsMultiple2022. Because both fits read the same imputations, the
imputation-side quantities are common to both and drop out of the difference, dataset by
dataset.

**The differential bias.** At the null, the residual bias of $\hat d_L$ relative to its
complete-data counterpart is
$\mathbb{E}[\hat d_L - \hat d_{\text{com}}] = \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp)$, the
missing information of the *tested* directions projected in the **complete-data** metric
$I_{\text{com}}$ — not in $I_{\text{obs}}$, because the Q-function's curvature is the
complete-data information, so the constrained fit projects there. This is **not** the naive
difference of the two models' self-contained trace corrections,
$\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_0)$, which always
*overstates* it: the gap has the closed form
$\operatorname{tr}[(I + H_{11})^{-1} H_{12} H_{12}^\top] \ge 0$ — the missing-information
coupling between the retained and tested directions in the standardized metric — and vanishes
exactly when the two subspaces carry independent missing information (the equal-fractions and
block-diagonal cases). The distinction is small in well-aligned designs but exceeds a factor
of three when the tested directions mix unequally-missing information.

**Pairing, and what survives.** Sharing the imputations does more than cancel a bias: it
collapses the per-dataset *noise* of the comparison from $O(\sqrt n)$ — the scale of each
model's standalone deviance bias — to $O(1)$, because the heavy-tailed realized constant that
drives that noise is literally the same realization in both fits and subtracts away. The same
sharing cancels the $O(1)$ design-imbalance term $(A)+(C)$ *exactly* at the null, leaves it
$o(1)$ under local alternatives, and lets it return as a genuine $O(1)$ only for non-nested
comparison, where the two models' pseudo-true values are separated at $O(1)$
[@vuongLikelihoodRatioTests1989]. A pre-registered measurement (§5) bears this out and
sharpens it: the per-model $(A)+(C)$ levels do decouple and the noise does return to
$O(\sqrt n)$, but the *differential* an information criterion would inherit is mild for
similar candidates and an appreciable $O(1)$ only for dissimilar ones.

**Where the bias bites.** At the null, $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_\perp)$ is exactly the
mean a correctly calibrated reference distribution already absorbs
[@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022], so a test that refers the *uncorrected* numerator to such a
reference is approximately calibrated — and the pre-registered rejection study confirms it
(§5). The differential bias matters instead where no reference stands between the statistic
and its consumer: information-criterion ranking, which compares penalized deviances as
numbers; explicit numerator corrections, which must use the projected trace rather than the
naive difference; and non-nested comparison, where both the design-imbalance differential and
the heavy-tailed noise return. The first of these is the subject of @sec-ic-app.

# Application II: information-criterion model selection {#sec-ic-app}

Model selection by information criterion has no reference distribution to hide behind: it
compares penalized averaged deviances *as numbers* and takes the smallest, so whatever bias
each candidate's $-2\bar Q_\infty$ carries lands directly in the ranking. By @thm-bias that
bias is $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k)$, computed on candidate $k$'s own parameter space,
and the corrected criterion is
$$ \mathrm{AIC}^c_{\mathrm{MI}}(k) = -2\bar Q_\infty(\hat\psi^*_k) + 2 q_k +
\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{RIV}_k). $$
As a penalty this is not new — it reproduces the missing-data information criterion
$\mathrm{AIC}_{x;y}$ of @shimodairaInformationCriterionModel2017, whose surcharge is exactly
half that of the complete-data-discrepancy criteria
[@cavanaughAkaikeInformationCriterion1998a]; what the present derivation adds is the *anatomy*
(the imputation-bias and estimation-mismatch split), the MAR design-imbalance term beyond the
fixed-pattern setting of those criteria, and the extension to proper MI. The ranking
consequence is immediate and directional: because the bias is model-specific, *uncorrected*
MI-AIC systematically favors the candidates with the **most** missing information about their
own parameters — and in the pre-registered study every single misclassification of the
uncorrected criterion lands on the largest-RIV candidate.

**Beyond the mean.** Selection depends on the *distribution* of pairwise criterion
differences, not just their means, so the sharper question is how much of that distribution a
correction can match. Anchoring each candidate's deviance against the saturated model gives,
at the candidate's own null, an analytic reference law — a weighted sum of chi-squares
$\sum_j \lambda_{k,j}\chi^2_1$ whose weights are the missing-information odds of the tested
directions [@chanGeneralFeasibleTests2022] — and three corrections of increasing ambition
follow from it. The **mean** correction subtracts the projected trace; the **two-moment**
correction applies the unique affine map matching the first two moments of that law to a
chi-square — the scaled and scaled-shifted statistics of the structural-equation tradition
[@satorraEnsuringPositivenessScaled2010; @asparouhovRobustChiSquare2006], but with
coefficients *derived* from the analytic law rather than estimated from plug-in moments; and
the **all-moments** correction equates the whole distribution through the probability integral
transform — equipercentile equating in the test-linking sense [@kolenTestEquatingScaling2014]
— evaluated in closed form through the Imhof distribution function
[@imhofComputingDistributionQuadratic1961; @daviesAlgorithm155Distribution1980].

**What matching achieves, and what it cannot.** Pre-registered with pass criteria frozen
before the code, the contrast splits cleanly along the null/noncentral axis (§5). On the null
side calibration is *complete*: the equated statistic is statistically indistinguishable from
its complete-data counterpart, and where selection errors are overfit flips the
distribution-matched criterion selects at the oracle rate. On the noncentral side no
observable transform can restore the evidence the missing data destroyed; the two stronger
corrections *shrink* it, by exactly the factors the analytic law predicts, and where the
signal is genuinely weak an information-loss floor binds that no correction closes. The honest
fences are stated bluntly and graded by their evidence: all-moment matching holds per pair
under that pair's own null and nowhere else; noncentral matching is impossible in principle and
per-model marginal equating cannot calibrate a difference distribution (both proven, the latter
by the invariance of the score copula under marginal maps); and none of this restores
complete-data selection where the data no longer carry the information (an empirical floor, not
a theorem). The non-nested $(A)+(C)$ differential of @sec-lrt-app is the remaining un-absorbed
bias here — real per candidate, mild for similar candidates, and an appreciable $O(1)$ for
dissimilar ones (§5).

# Numerical studies {#sec-numerics}

All studies are pre-registered, with predictions and pass criteria frozen before execution;
the pre-registration documents, amendment history, seeds, and committed caches are in the
repository (`todo/004`, `todo/027`, `todo/029`; `verification/`). The companion derivation
carries the full grading; what follows states what each study found.

- **Theorem validation (W1).** Design, the heavy-tail caveat, the pooled reconciliation
  with `todo/004`'s Amendment 2 (the criterion note) — honest framing: the per-cell criterion
  was written against the half-trace alone before the $O(1)$ MAR term was derived; the pooled
  estimate
  corroborates the completed theorem. Pointers: `verification/W1-bias-decomposition.R`
  (frozen), `verification/w1-h1-pooled-reconciliation.R`.
- **Likelihood-ratio power and calibration (W2).** The four-arm rejection study; the
  near-nominal uncorrected null rate against a calibrated reference (0.042) and the
  slightly conservative corrected one (0.034), as the theory predicts; the
  size-adjusted-power analysis. The scaled-shift comparison arm (C5) is presented strictly
  as an *empirical two-moment-matching arm*: its committed coefficients used the naive
  trace difference as input, which Proposition L2 shows overstates the exact complement
  trace (immaterial in this near-aligned design, real in general) — the *derived*
  two-moment correction is the model-selection contrast's second step, where its
  coefficients come from the analytic null law rather than from plug-in moments.
- **Model selection (W3 + the matching contrast).** The committed 60-cell sweep (oracle
  $\approx 0.90$ / corrected $\approx 0.81$ / uncorrected $\approx 0.65$; all uncorrected
  misclassifications on the largest-RIV candidate); the pre-registered three-step contrast
  at $\rho \in \{0.40, 0.10, 0\}$ with every criterion passed — null side complete
  calibration, oracle-level selection where errors are overfit flips, the predicted
  noncentral shrinkage factors, the weak-signal information-loss floor. Pointers:
  `verification/cache/run_all-prod/phase2-w3/summary.csv`,
  `verification/cache/ic-ladder-summary.csv`, `todo/027-ladder-preregistration.md`.
- **Non-nested design-imbalance differential (W4).** A pre-registered measurement
  ($N \in \{500, 1000, 2000\}$, $20{,}000$ replicates per cell, an MCAR twin for mechanism
  isolation) of the only derived-but-previously-unmeasured quantity — the $(A)+(C)$ differential of
  Proposition L4 for a genuine non-nested (Vuong) pair, compound symmetry versus AR(1). It confirms
  the *mechanism*: each candidate's single-model $(A)+(C)$ level is a large $O(1)$ ($\approx +2.5$
  deviance units, tens of standard errors) that collapses to zero under MCAR, the two levels
  decouple, and the paired noise returns to $O(\sqrt n)$ — for *any* pseudo-true-separated pair, a
  nested-but-false restriction included, so it is separation rather than nesting that triggers the
  return. It *refines* the magnitude: for this similar pair the two large levels nearly cancel,
  leaving the differential below resolution, so the un-absorbed bias that reaches an information
  criterion is real per candidate but mild for similar candidates and grows with dissimilarity. That
  scaling is then *anchored*, not merely asserted: a pre-registered dissimilar-pair follow-up
  (Amendment 2 — a structure-blind diagonal model versus AR(1)) carries an isolated design-imbalance
  differential that is a resolved $O(1)$ ($-1.2$ at $-3.5$ se, three-to-four times the similar pair's
  and un-trending), with the honest rider that a badly misspecified candidate also carries a
  mechanism-independent misspecification $O(1)$ which the MCAR contrast separates and which can
  partially offset the net ranking bias. A further byproduct is the first in-vivo case off
  Proposition L2's equality — the naive trace difference overstates the exact complement trace by
  $\approx 9\%$ per curved candidate. So the *similar*-pair headline (a resolved nonzero differential)
  was not met — that difference is below resolution — while the dissimilar follow-up was; both are
  reported per the pre-registration discipline. Pointers:
  `todo/029-nonnested-preregistration.md` (§6 + Amendment 2 grading),
  `verification/w4-nonnested-vuong.R`, `verification/w4-dissimilar-vuong.R`,
  `verification/cas-wolfram/verify_w4_nonnested.py`.

# Discussion {#sec-disc}

The result is scoped by its own mechanism. The bias is present only when the fit estimates a
scale or covariance, so that the conditional missing-data entropy depends on the estimated
parameters; a known-scale, location-only fit has no such bias, which is why the estimated-scale
clause is in the theorem rather than a footnote. We instantiate throughout with the
multivariate normal, where every quantity is closed form: the decomposition is stated generally
but its constants are MVN. Congeniality is assumed throughout — the imputer's and analyst's
models agree — and what the bias becomes without it
[@mengMultipleImputationInferencesUncongenial1994a] is a separate question we do not take up
here. The pre-registered studies use deterministic-FIML and EMB imputation, which agree within
$0.011$ on the headline selection rates; data-augmentation engines were not run, an honestly
disclosed gap rather than a claim of engine-invariance.

Two fronts are left open. The first is a power-dominance question — whether the bias-corrected
likelihood-ratio comparison dominates its uncorrected counterpart uniformly — which we state as
a **conjecture only**, with the W2 power study as partial support. The second is the non-nested
regime. There the design-imbalance differential, which cancels at the null and is negligible
under local alternatives, returns as a genuine $O(1)$; the pre-registered measurement (§5)
confirms the mechanism — the per-model levels decouple and the paired noise reverts to
$O(\sqrt n)$ — and anchors the magnitude at both ends of the similarity axis: below resolution
for a similar candidate pair, an appreciable $O(1)$ for a dissimilar one. That measurement also
surfaced a distinction worth its own study: a misspecified candidate carries, alongside the
MAR-specific design-imbalance term, a mechanism-independent misspecification $O(1)$, and the two
can reinforce or partially offset in the net bias a criterion sees. Mapping that interaction
across candidate space, and the distributional-calibration program beyond per-model null maps —
noncentrality de-shrinkage, joint calibration of score vectors — is the sequel.

# Provenance and authorship statement {#sec-provenance}

This is an AI-assisted derivation with a human prompter of record (Marcus Waldman,
ORCID-verified), prepared for the Journal for AI Generated Papers under its transparency
norms. The provenance stack is part of the submission: the companion sourced derivation
(`manuscript/derivation.qmd`) pins every external claim to a verbatim source passage in a
locally archived copy and every number to a committed artifact; the `verification/`
directory contains the pre-registered studies, their frozen originals, amendment history,
and caches; analytic claims were additionally cross-checked by an independent model
lineage under a blind adversarial protocol, with the grading records committed. Citation
discipline is enforced mechanically: no reference appears in this manuscript without a
locally archived, session-read copy of the source.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
